title: How Limestone Caves Form Over Millennia

lesson.learn:
Limestone caves form when slightly acidic water dissolves rock over extremely long periods of time. Rain picks up carbon dioxide from air and soil, becomes weak carbonic acid, and slowly widens cracks in limestone. As those cracks grow, they become passages, chambers, and entire cave systems. Later, mineral-rich water dripping through the cave can build stalactites, stalagmites, and flowstone.

Caves are therefore both carved and decorated by water. They are geological spaces and chemical archives at the same time.
lesson.simpler: Limestone caves form when acidic water slowly dissolves rock, then deposits minerals as drips and formations.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Caves are made by dissolution first and decoration later.
lesson.deeperDive:
The process depends on karst chemistry. Limestone is soluble enough that groundwater can enlarge cracks and joints over time, especially where water keeps moving through the same pathways. Once a cave exists, the chemistry changes: dripping water loses carbon dioxide and leaves behind calcite. That is how delicate speleothems grow one tiny layer at a time.

Because the process is so slow, caves are records of enormous patience. A single cave system can reflect climate, water flow, and rock chemistry over huge stretches of time.
lesson.funFact: Most cave formations grow far more slowly than trees.

people: Cave explorers - people who map underground systems
places: Karst regions - where limestone caves are common
Limestone bedrock - the rock caves dissolve in
Cave chambers - the spaces created underground
years: Present day - caves are studied as climate archives
concepts: Karst - terrain shaped by dissolving rock
Calcite - the mineral that forms many cave features
Stalactite - a formation hanging from the ceiling
Stalagmite - a formation growing from the floor
